title: Confit de Canard
subtitle: Duck legs cured in salt overnight, then slow-cooked submerged in their own fat
description: Confit de canard is one of the great preservation techniques of Gascon cooking: duck legs salted overnight, rinsed, then cooked at the lowest possible temperature submerged in rendered duck fat for two to three hours until completely tender. The result is duck that pulls effortlessly from the bone, with skin that crisps to a shard under a hot grill or in a hot pan in minutes. Made in advance and stored in the fat, confit keeps for weeks in the fridge. It is effort concentrated at the front, rewarded with very little work at serving time.
sourceNotes: Confit de canard is the signature technique of Gascony in southwestern France, where preserved duck has been made since at least the medieval period. Escoffier's Le Guide Culinaire (1903) codifies the salt-cure and fat-submerge method. The technique appears in Paula Wolfert's The Cooking of South-West France (1983), the definitive English-language source for Gascon cookery.

recipeTools.notes: Deep enough to hold the duck legs submerged in fat — a 5–7 cm deep ceramic or cast-iron dish.
For crisping the skin at serving time.
To verify the fat temperature during confit — the fat should be 80–90 °C throughout.

glossaryTerms.definition: A French preservation method: meat (typically duck, goose, or pork) is salted, then cooked at a very low temperature submerged in its own rendered fat. The fat seals the cooked meat from air, preserving it for weeks.

The application of salt (and sometimes sugar and aromatics) to raw meat, left for hours or days. Salt draws out moisture, seasons the flesh deeply, and in this context seasons the duck throughout rather than just on the surface.

ingredients.notes: Enough to submerge the legs — available in tins or jars

steps: The night before: scatter the salt, thyme, bay leaves, garlic, and cracked pepper over the duck legs on both sides. Cover and refrigerate overnight, at least 12 hours.

The next day: remove the duck legs from the fridge and brush off the salt, thyme, bay, and garlic. Rinse briefly under cold water and pat completely dry. Do not skip the drying — surface moisture affects the fat temperature and skin texture.
Heat the oven to 120 °C fan (140 °C / gas 1). Melt the duck fat in an ovenproof dish over a low hob heat.
Lower the duck legs into the fat — they should be completely submerged, or as close to it as possible. If the fat does not cover the legs, add a little more melted fat or olive oil.

Transfer the dish to the oven. The fat temperature should be between 80–90 °C throughout — check with a thermometer after 30 minutes and adjust the oven temperature if needed. Cook for 2.5–3 hours until the duck meat is very tender and pulls away from the bone at the slightest pressure.
The confit can be served immediately or stored: leave the legs in the fat, allow to cool to room temperature, cover, and refrigerate for up to 3 weeks. The fat seals and preserves the duck.

To serve: lift the duck legs out of the fat, scraping off any excess. Heat a heavy frying pan or oven grill to high. Place the legs skin-side down in the pan (or skin-side up under the grill). Cook for 4–6 minutes until the skin is deeply crisp and golden. Serve immediately — skin up.

content.text: The overnight salt cure is where the flavour goes in. The low-temperature

in fat is where the texture is built. The final crisping of the skin at high heat is where the drama happens — and that part takes under ten minutes. Plan the timeline: salt the evening before, cook the next afternoon, serve that evening or store for up to three weeks.

{{duck-leg}} of duck legs: scatter {{flaky-sea-salt}} of flaky salt over every surface, along with the crushed garlic, thyme sprigs, bay leaves, and cracked black pepper. Cover and refrigerate for at least 12 hours.

Rinse the cure off the duck legs, pat completely dry. Heat the oven to 120 °C fan. Melt {{duck-fat}} of duck fat in a deep ovenproof dish on the hob. Lower the duck legs into the fat — they should be submerged. Transfer to the oven and cook for 2.5–3 hours, checking with a thermometer that the fat stays at 80–90 °C. The duck is ready when the meat pulls from the bone with no resistance.

Lift the legs from the fat and wipe off the excess. Heat a heavy frying pan to high or set the grill to maximum. Place the legs skin-side down (or up under the grill) for 4–6 minutes until the skin is deeply crisp and shatteringly golden. Serve immediately, skin-side up.

Leave the cooked duck legs in the fat, cool to room temperature, then transfer to a lidded container — legs submerged — and refrigerate. The fat solidifies around the duck and acts as a seal, keeping the confit in good condition for up to 3 weeks. To retrieve a leg, warm the container briefly to melt the surrounding fat, lift the leg out, scrape it clean, and crisp the skin as above.
